A double lumen tube is utilized.

Next, a right subclavian venous catheter is placed.
This is proved most comfortable for the patient postoperatively.
The patient is then turned in the right side up position and using an Allen arm holder, the right arm is comfortably positioned.
A bean bag is then positioned.
And the patient is rotated slightly towards the back.
so the patient is not in a true 90 degree position.

The pad on the back is placed between the shoulder blades
These pads are also used for the EKG pickups for the OR lab.

This is available from the Scanlon company under the name of Wolf Pediatric Yankower.
I prefer the Castro-Diego type endoscopic instruments as they afford fine control to the surgeon during this procedure.
In addition to checking the chest x-ray, it's important to note the topical anatomy.
We make a line at the xiphoid or in women at the inframammary fold and we mark the midaxillary line.
A line from the xiphoid posteriorly crosses the midaxillary line and where this cross occurs is usually a good position for the first working port.
If the patient is morbidly obese, we may move the incision site for the first port one interspace cephalad.
Here's a view from the head of the patient with the same markings.

And then we begin with an incision for the first working port.
Caudry is used.
Always use a blunt port, do not use a sharp edged trocar.
Now that we're inside the chest, we can palpate the chest wall and document that indeed our mark for our incision is at the fourth anacostal space.
In some instances, in obese patients, the third intercostal space lines up over the hilum.
But in most patients, fourth intercostal space works quite well.
This is a six centimeter incision in the intercostal space.
We generally don't spread the ribs.
We use a soft tissue retractor, which you'll see.
Meticulous use of the cotter is important.
We want absolutely no blood dripping into the chest wall during this procedure.
The anatomy is much better seen without any blood staining.
Now the fat is retracted posteriorly with my index finger, left index finger,
This is an important move.
This pushes any cutaneous nerves posteriorly.
We do not cut into that fat posteriorly.
From the inside the chest here you can see we're opening directly over the fifth rib.
Of course it's important to open directly over the fifth rib.
We turn the cautery
on 30 for this part of the procedure.
We do not have the cartilage on a high level such as 50 or 60.
That can also cause intercostal nerve damage.
The key is to avoid injury to the nerves on entry into the chest.
You can see that the incision is clean and dry.
This is visualized externally as well as internally.
The soft tissue retractor is placed in an X as opposed to a cross configuration to best visualize through the six centimeter working port.
The first instrument that I usually use is a sponge stick.
This is to retract the lung.
If there are any adhesions, they should be divided.
And this usually allows us direct visualization of the phrenic nerve.
And here you see the right phrenic nerve running in a little fat pad on the posterior portion of the pericardium.
One must be vigilant of this nerve at all times and one of the jobs of our camera operator is to alert the surgeon if the surgeon is anywhere near the nerve.
The cautery is on 30 for opening the skin and subcutaneous and muscle.
Then we turn the cautery down to 15.
Again, to decrease the chance of any lateral thermal spread that could get close to the phrenic nerve.
The endoscopic kidney, which you see here in the screen, is used to depress the diaphragm.
This is particularly helpful in obese patients and is very effective in obtaining visualization.
We open the pericardium with the cautery on 15 to 20.
This is monopolar RF.
And we open the pericardium a few centimeters medial to the right phrenic nerve.
One has to be careful about not touching the phrenic nerve either with the cautery device or with the graspers or inadvertently picking up the pericardium adjacent to the right phrenic nerve.
We always have at least two set of eyes on the phrenic nerve.
Here you can see in this patient the diaphragm is running fairly high.
with an endoscopic kitner placed through the working port.
The diaphragm can be depressed and this greatly improves visualization of the right side of the pericardium.
When working more superiorly, we sometimes place an Army-Navy on the top side of the working port to improve direct visualization towards the superior aspect of this incision.
When there is abundant pericardial fat, some of the pericardial fat is resected.
Superiorly, this pericardial fat can be divided.
So the keys are to resect pericardial fat anteriorly.
Of course, don't resect it posteriorly because that could be close to the right phrenic nerve.
Divide the pericardial fat superiorly, as you see here, and always have direct visualization of the area of the phrenic nerve.
Pericardial stays are placed laterally in the cut edge of the pericardium, usually two but occasionally three may need to be placed for visualization.
Care again must be taken to identify the phrenic nerve and keep the pericardial stay from the phrenic nerve edge.
These are then brought out through stab incisions through the skin in an appropriate place.
The pericardial stays need to be taut, but not overly tightened.
If they're tightened too much, they would pull through.
In this case, you see we have two pericardial stays, one at about the level of the beginning of the superior vena cava, and one quite low, close to the inferior pulmonary vein.
And there you can see where they're exiting in the intercostal spaces.
The pericardium can be opened a bit more superiorly if needed.

1200 beats per minute.

We also test the inferior pulmonary vein and document that there is a complete block.
In order to facilitate treating all the GPs, I initially did this well before we had any pin.

In this particular case, this patient has long-standing persistent atrial fibrillation, and we're performing an inferior crossing lesion.

Using the bipolar pen, we're creating RF energy and every 10 seconds there's a change in the sound of the signal and we move the pen.
This is called the stamping technique.
This is a technique we demonstrated in the laboratory that works the best for 4 to 5 millimeters of penetration on the atrial wall.
So you hold the pen in one place for 10 seconds
and then move it a couple centimeters and hold it again until a complete line is fashioned.
